title: Some New Beginnings

jokes.text: Is this a Star Wars reference, 'A New Beginning'? / Our Next American Journey.
jokes.setup: Someone notices the book title sounds like a Star Wars subtitle and asks if it's intentional.
jokes.punchline: Selina flatly corrects them — the title is 'Our Next American Journey,' which is equally grandiose and meaningless, making the correction itself the joke.
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient jab at the vacuousness of political book titles. The joke lands because both the Star Wars comparison AND the actual title are embarrassing, so the correction offers no rescue.

jokes.text: Too late to change it? / It is. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina has just heard the title compared to Star Wars and clearly hates it.
jokes.punchline: She asks if it's too late to change the title of her already-printed book, and is flatly told yes — revealing she's been doing this whole tour behind a title she despises.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical. Two words carry a lot of character — Selina's self-awareness undercut by her powerlessness. The flat 'It is. Yeah.' from the staffer is perfect deadpan.

jokes.text: I call it Some New Beginnings because it's plural.
jokes.setup: Selina is at a book signing doing her promotional patter, listing bullet points of her platform.
jokes.punchline: She defends the title with the feeblest possible justification — it's plural — as if that alone redeems the meaningless phrase.
jokes.explanation: Sharp piece of character comedy — Selina sells political emptiness with total conviction. The defense is so thin it becomes an indictment. Very Veep-specific: politicians who have no idea what they're saying but say it with authority.

jokes.text: I would hate to be that local Iowa guy that's gotta take care of her, trying to source gazpacho in a city that thinks soup is for fags.
jokes.setup: Mike is at his wedding speaking warmly about Selina being in Iowa on the book tour.
jokes.punchline: He pivots into a savagely specific and inappropriate complaint about regional food politics and a homophobic punchline — completely inappropriate for the wedding context but revealing about DC insider condescension.
jokes.explanation: Funny for the specificity of 'gazpacho' and the DC-insider contempt for flyover America. The slur is played as Mike being obliviously offensive rather than the show endorsing it, which is classic Veep cringe. The gazpacho detail is the star.

jokes.text: Nice day for a Mike wedding!
jokes.setup: Mike is talking enthusiastically at his own wedding.
jokes.punchline: He riffs on 'Nice Day for a White Wedding' by Billy Idol, substituting his own name — delivered with the self-congratulatory glee of someone who thinks this is extremely clever.
jokes.explanation: The pun itself is weak, but it works as character comedy — this is exactly the kind of dad-joke Mike would make with maximum enthusiasm. The joke is Mike's obliviousness, not the pun.

jokes.text: Would you like me to mold the cake into a pair of testicles for you, Gary?
jokes.setup: Gary is hovering anxiously, telling Mike he did the playlist, making the entire wedding about himself.
jokes.punchline: Mike snaps back with a surreally specific offer to humiliate Gary — mold the cake into testicles — combining aggression, absurdity, and the implication that Gary needs to grow some.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Veep insult comedy — specific, unexpected, and perfectly targeted. 'Mold the cake into a pair of testicles' is so specific it transcends generic insults. Character-integrated: Mike's barely-contained exasperation at Gary's neediness.

jokes.text: Yeah, dream the fuck on, Dan.
jokes.setup: Dan makes the sentimental observation that without Selina, they're like parents whose kids have gone to college.
jokes.punchline: Amy destroys the sentiment with four words and a profanity, revealing she has zero warmth for Dan and his attempts at camaraderie.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words doing heavy lifting. Amy's contempt for Dan's faux-sentimentality is totally in character. 'Dream the fuck on' is a memorable inversion of the cliché 'dream on' that adds aggression perfectly calibrated to the character dynamic.

jokes.text: I'm only being nice to you because I know that Selina's gonna make me the campaign manager.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are in a tense détente at the wedding.
jokes.punchline: Dan openly admits his niceness is entirely strategic — destroying any pretense of civility and revealing his sociopathic political calculus with total transparency.
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy. Dan's willingness to say the quiet part loud is his defining trait, and this line encapsulates it perfectly. The absurdity is that he thinks this is a reasonable thing to say aloud.

jokes.text: Unless POTUS is chiseling his fucking statement out of marble, I don't get it.
jokes.setup: The team is frustrated that the president hasn't yet announced he's not running, two months after telling Selina.
jokes.punchline: Dan's hyperbolic image of the president literally chiseling his statement from marble captures the absurd delay with a specific, vivid metaphor.
jokes.explanation: Good image comedy. 'Chiseling out of marble' is a specific and funny elaboration on the frustration. The profanity amplifies the impatience authentically. Classic Veep walk-and-talk hyperbole.

jokes.text: Dan, Dan, Dan! Inside voices. Mike's marrying a reporter. They're everywhere.
jokes.setup: Dan is loudly speculating about POTUS's announcement at a wedding filled with journalists.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'inside voices' — a phrase used to discipline children — applied to Dan, then the paranoid follow-up 'They're everywhere' like a spy thriller.
jokes.explanation: The patronizing 'inside voices' directed at a grown adult is funny, and 'They're everywhere' adds a nice absurdist paranoia beat. Double-function: establishes Wendy is a reporter which pays off later.

jokes.text: Wow, look at you, Dan. You have more nervous tics than a shoe-bomber.
jokes.setup: Dan is visibly anxious and fidgety about the political news breaking.
jokes.punchline: Amy compares his nervous energy to a shoe-bomber — a wildly specific and dark comparison that is both an insult and an absurd image.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout insults. 'More nervous tics than a shoe-bomber' is genuinely original — it's specific, dark, and perfectly calibrated Veep insult comedy. The image of a shoe-bomber being nervous is funny on its own; applying it to Dan's political anxiety is excellent craft. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: In the shape of the great state of Iowa. / If it melted, would it become Texas?
jokes.setup: Selina is presented with an impressive butter sculpture of Iowa.
jokes.punchline: Ben deadpans: if it melted, would it become Texas? — implying Texas is a formless, inferior blob, a perfect DC insider contempt joke.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. The logic is precise — if the butter sculpture of Iowa melted, the resulting formless mass 'becomes' Texas — simultaneously a geographic insult to Texas AND an absurdist observation about melting states. It arrives instantly, is perfectly economical, and is the kind of dry joke you think about for a moment before it hits. Ben's deadpan delivery elevates it further.

jokes.text: You know, butter doesn't need to be refrigerated. It can be unrefrigerated or refrigerated. / That's not true. / I think it's like a fruit that way.
jokes.setup: The team is trying to preserve the butter sculpture and someone suggests refrigerating it.
jokes.punchline: Richard confidently asserts wrong food science, then doubles down with the baffling analogy 'it's like a fruit that way' — revealing him as confidently incompetent.
jokes.explanation: Richard's establishing character joke. The three-beat structure is tight: confident wrong claim → flat correction → doubling down with an even more baffling analogy. 'It's like a fruit that way' is the kind of absurdist non-sequitur that becomes a cult quote. Strong character introduction.

jokes.text: like a fly in a shithouse
jokes.setup: Amy is relaying rumors about a major announcement from the West Wing.
jokes.punchline: She describes the West Wing as 'abuzz like a fly in a shithouse' — a disgusting, specific, and perfectly deflating metaphor for Washington power.
jokes.explanation: A well-chosen piece of invective. 'Abuzz like a fly in a shithouse' is a classically rude simile that degrades Washington's self-importance. The contrast between 'West Wing' prestige and 'shithouse' is the joke.

jokes.text: Either you put your phone in the phone bowl or I put it in the punch bowl. / Isn't she funny? We crack up all the time. Even when we're having sex.
jokes.setup: Wendy is insisting Amy surrenders her phone into a phone bowl for the ceremony.
jokes.punchline: Mike TMI-endorses Wendy's personality by adding they laugh during sex — an overshare that transforms wedding warmth into cringe.
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke: the phone bowl ultimatum is functional setup, but Mike's spontaneous overshare about laughing during sex is the real laugh. Character-specific — Mike is the kind of oblivious person who says this.

jokes.text: It's like losing a limb. I can feel a phantom phone ringing right now.
jokes.setup: Amy reluctantly surrenders her phone into the bowl, visibly traumatized.
jokes.punchline: She describes the loss as phantom limb syndrome — specifically a 'phantom phone ringing' — a modern anxiety joke delivered as genuine suffering.
jokes.explanation: The phantom limb analogy is well-chosen and feels fresh applied to phone separation anxiety. Amy's absolute sincerity about this being equivalent to losing a body part is perfect character work. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Is that lip shade coral blush? / Yes, it is. Wow. / Nicely applied. / I like him. I want to keep him. Can I have him, please?
jokes.setup: Gary is speaking to Wendy, Mike's new wife, who is apparently impressed by Gary's makeup knowledge.
jokes.punchline: Selina hearing this from Iowa immediately wants to 'keep' the Gary-like person Wendy has found — revealing Gary's role as Selina's personal accessory and her possessiveness about that niche.
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Gary's lip shade knowledge is absurdly specific and Selina's immediate 'can I have him' treats a human being as a household item. It's a running theme about Gary's infantilization played lightly here.

jokes.text: 'He does bring baby wipes everywhere. You never know if we're gonna need 'em when we... Okay. Got it.'
jokes.setup: Selina is explaining why she wants to keep the makeup person, mentioning his baby wipes
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts herself off mid-sentence when she realizes what she's implying — the self-censored admission
jokes.explanation: The interrupted thought — 'when we...' — works as both a character revelation and a cringe beat. The 'Okay. Got it.' suggests whoever she's talking to quickly understood. Selina's sexuality glimpsed awkwardly through campaign logistics is very Veep.

jokes.text: A good campaign manager always has to think one phone ahead. / You're not a campaign manager yet.
jokes.setup: Dan is caught using a secret spare phone he hid from the phone bowl.
jokes.punchline: He justifies it as campaign manager thinking; Amy deflates him with 'you're not a campaign manager yet' — both their rivalry and his delusion encapsulated.
jokes.explanation: Efficient two-line exchange that crystallizes the Dan-Amy dynamic. Dan's self-aggrandizing justification and Amy's flat correction — 'you're not a campaign manager yet' — is a perfect beat. Character integration is the strength here.

jokes.text: Yes, and I likewise am disappointed that you have not. Amateur.
jokes.setup: Amy expresses disappointment that Dan hid a spare phone.
jokes.punchline: Dan flips it: he's disappointed AMY didn't also hide a spare phone, calling her an 'amateur' — inverting moral judgment entirely.
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character integration. The moral inversion — the wrongdoer disappointed the accuser didn't also break the rules — is very Dan. 'Amateur' lands as both an insult and a worldview. One of Dan's best moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Yes, it is a wonderful day today.
jokes.setup: Selina calls Mike, clearly wanting intelligence about the Maddox situation, and Mike is at his own wedding.
jokes.punchline: Mike responds to what is clearly a coded 'is anything happening?' with pure wedding-host banality — either obliviously or deliberately keeping it neutral in front of guests.
jokes.explanation: Understated but effective — Mike's code-switching at his own wedding, unable to be a normal person even on his wedding day, is a recurring theme. The mundane reply to a political inquiry is a small character moment.

jokes.text: It happened so fast. Mike just makes me laugh. As a matter of fact, the first time we met, I was carrying this cheese Danish and... / Then you got married. And I love that story.
jokes.setup: Wendy starts telling Selina the story of how she and Mike met.
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts her off mid-sentence and announces 'then you got married. And I love that story.' — having fabricated an ending and declared it over with zero interest in the actual story.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Selina's 'Then you got married. And I love that story.' is pitch-perfect character comedy — her utter inability to feign interest in other people's lives, the confidence of the fabricated ending, and the cheerful 'I love that story' about an anecdote she refused to hear. This is writing at the level of the 8+ anchor: it's specific to this character, beautifully structured, highly quotable, and lands hard. The cheese Danish detail makes Wendy's story more poignant by comparison.

jokes.text: you have to suckle Iowa
jokes.setup: Mike is explaining to Selina why she can't be at his wedding — she's in Iowa.
jokes.punchline: 'You have to suckle Iowa' — a viscerally physical metaphor for political pandering that makes the relationship between candidate and state grotesque.
jokes.explanation: 'Suckle Iowa' is a very specific and evocative insult at the political process disguised as a helpful statement. The word choice — 'suckle' rather than 'court' or 'charm' — does all the work. Typical of Veep's ability to make political cynicism visceral.

jokes.text: 'You have to suckle Iowa.' — Wendy's description of Selina's political obligations
jokes.setup: Wendy is explaining to Selina why she couldn't attend the wedding
jokes.punchline: 'Suckle Iowa' — an unexpectedly visceral and nursing-related metaphor for political pandering
jokes.explanation: 'Suckle Iowa' is genuinely original — the political-as-nursing metaphor is unexpected, slightly gross, and accurate. The economy is excellent. Docked slightly because it's not character-specific (could be said by anyone).

jokes.text: Mike is not. / Okay. Bye-bye. Thanks.
jokes.setup: Selina has finished pretending to care about Wendy and now wants to talk business with Mike.
jokes.punchline: Wendy coldly informs her Mike is gone. Selina, without missing a beat: 'Okay. Bye-bye. Thanks.' — dismissing a bride on her wedding day with the tone of canceling a food delivery.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — 'Okay. Bye-bye. Thanks.' is the sound of Selina ending a business transaction, not a warm conversation with a newlywed. The abrupt pivot from performative warmth to instant dismissal is classic Selina character comedy.

jokes.text: After Wendy is handed the phone by Mike, Selina asks to speak to Mike again immediately. Mike has left. 'Okay. Bye-bye. Thanks.' Then instantly: 'Fuck.'
jokes.setup: Selina went through the social niceties of speaking to Wendy, only to discover she can't get back to Mike
jokes.punchline: The whiplash from 'Bye-bye. Thanks.' to 'Fuck.' — the polished VP performance and the reality slamming together
jokes.explanation: The gap between the performative farewell and the immediate profanity is a tight comic rhythm. The mask dropping in an instant is very Selina. Economy is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: What's your favorite word? / Next!
jokes.setup: Selina is exhausted at her book signing and an audience member tries to engage her personally.
jokes.punchline: Asked what her favorite word is, Selina shouts 'Next!' — both an answer and a command to move the line along, revealing her utter contempt for the signing.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect economy. 'Next!' as both favorite word and dismissal is a double-meaning joke that works flawlessly. It's specific to Selina's contempt for constituent engagement, completely in character, and instantly quotable. Structure is excellent — the setup question is genuine, making the punchline more surprising.

jokes.text: Gary's cover story for Selina leaving: 'The Vice President has to step out for a moment to take a phone call with a senator. He's having a big problem. It's...'
jokes.setup: Gary has been asked to make up an excuse for Selina's absence
jokes.punchline: Gary's improvised lie keeps getting more specific and more absurd, trailing off as he digs in
jokes.explanation: Gary's improvisational lying — adding unnecessary details and then trailing off — is characteristic. The blank '...It's...' as he flounders is a nice beat.

jokes.text: Gary just usually makes up an excuse. / Uh, ladies and gentlemen, the Vice President has to step out for a moment to take a phone call with a senator. He's having a big problem.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Gary she's leaving and asks him to make up an excuse to the crowd.
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'excuse' is absurdly over-detailed — 'a phone call with a senator... He's having a big problem' — adding unnecessary specificity that makes it more suspicious, not less.
jokes.explanation: Good Gary moment — he is constitutionally unable to lie cleanly, so he adds 'He's having a big problem' because he thinks it sells it. The specificity is his undoing and perfectly characterizes his eager incompetence.

jokes.text: I am but a satellite caught in the gravity of your planet, Wendy.
jokes.setup: Mike is delivering his wedding vows.
jokes.punchline: The metaphor is sincere but physically unflattering to Wendy — comparing her to a 'planet' whose gravity traps satellites — delivered with total earnestness, making it worse.
jokes.explanation: The unintentional body-shaming of calling your bride a 'planet' during wedding vows is a beautifully Veep-style cringe joke. Mike is trying to be romantic; the metaphor is accidentally insulting. His earnestness makes it hurt-laugh territory.

jokes.text: My official statement to the press reads thus, 'We are in love, come celebrate.'
jokes.setup: Wendy is delivering her wedding vows and has been referencing love poetry.
jokes.punchline: She frames her vow as an 'official statement to the press' — Wendy is a reporter and can only express love in press-release language, revealing her professional identity consuming her personal one.
jokes.explanation: Strong character-specific joke for Wendy. The press-release framing of wedding vows is funny and says something real about journalists — but it's also a Veep-world joke where even love is mediated through official communications. Character integration is the best element.

jokes.text: Gel out of the way, or I'll fucking inhale you.
jokes.setup: Ben is trying to get past Richard, who is in his way.
jokes.punchline: 'I'll fucking inhale you' — a threat that replaces physical violence with the image of Ben absorbing Richard like a whale eating krill, which is both more threatening and more absurd.
jokes.explanation: 'I'll fucking inhale you' is a great line — the verb choice is unexpected and more visceral than generic threats. It implies complete absorption/annihilation of a smaller person, which is both funny and characterizes Ben's bulldozing presence perfectly.

jokes.text: He was a 4'11" stick of dynamite. / A great man inside a small man.
jokes.setup: Ben and Selina are discussing the late Congressman Cowgill at his funeral.
jokes.punchline: They eulogize him as 'a great man inside a small man' — which is both a touching sentiment AND a confirmation that he was indeed very short, making the tribute work as both sincere and comic.
jokes.explanation: Nicely constructed — 'a great man inside a small man' works on multiple levels. The height joke is well-concealed inside genuine-sounding tribute language. Sets up the giant cigar gag that follows.

jokes.text: Stick one of those in Ricky's hand, he would have looked like an average-sized man.
jokes.setup: Ben references a large cigar or prop while discussing the diminutive late congressman.
jokes.punchline: Holding a large object would make Cowgill look average-sized by proportion — a casually cruel height joke at a dead man's funeral, delivered as a fond memory.
jokes.explanation: Morbidly specific and funny. The logic — give a small man a big object so he looks normal-sized — is weirdly precise and delivered with the warmth of a eulogy, making it both dark and affectionate. Tone-deaf fondness is the joke.

jokes.text: Oh, they're at Mike's wedding. / I kinda miss 'em. / Oh, it's just Gary. Press ignore.
jokes.setup: Selina mentions she misses her team. Her phone buzzes immediately.
jokes.punchline: Sees it's Gary — the first of the team to reach out — and immediately says 'press ignore.' The longing for connection evaporates the moment the specific person makes contact.
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic reversal in three beats: (1) 'I miss 'em' — genuine warmth, (2) phone rings, (3) 'Press ignore.' The speed of the reversal is what makes it. Selina's relationship with Gary — she needs him but can't stand him — encapsulated in ten seconds. Rewatch bonus because you see it coming on second viewing and it's even funnier.

jokes.text: All right, all you single ladies... I don't wanna do it.
jokes.setup: The bouquet toss is announced at Mike's wedding.
jokes.punchline: Amy is presumably positioned for the bouquet toss and flatly announces she doesn't want to do it — breaking wedding tradition's pretense of fun with pure honesty.
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Amy's inability to perform social niceties is consistent. The joke is the abruptness, but it's not among the episode's strongest moments. Works better as rhythm than standalone comedy.

jokes.text: I got a jet here that could cut a fucking diamond.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives at the wedding uninvited, making his entrance.
jokes.punchline: He describes his flatulence as a 'jet that could cut a diamond' — introducing himself with a boast about his gas with total confidence.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Jonah — he introduces himself with a fart joke delivered with the swagger of a man announcing military power. 'Could cut a diamond' as a fart description is specific and funny. Character integration is the strength.

jokes.text: What the hell are you doing here? You weren't invited. / Unless you're the worst man.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives uninvited at the wedding.
jokes.punchline: Amy quips 'unless you're the worst man' — a pun on 'best man' that is also a genuine insult to Jonah's character.
jokes.explanation: Tight wordplay with genuine bite. 'Worst man' as an inversion of 'best man' is both a pun and a character assessment, doing double work in two words. Economy is the standout element.

jokes.text: Pentagon Pam? / She give you any hint on the Maddox announcement? / Nah, she didn't give me anything. Except for flirty glances.
jokes.setup: Jonah claims he drove a Pentagon contact to the wedding.
jokes.punchline: He offers 'flirty glances' as evidence of a meaningful connection — completely delusional about both the intelligence value and the romance.
jokes.explanation: Functional Jonah character joke — his delusional self-assessment applied simultaneously to intelligence-gathering AND romance. The 'except for flirty glances' is a good pivot but not among the episode's stronger moments.

jokes.text: What's that stubby thing you got there? / 'WestWingMan.net'? Never heard of this. / Come on, man. You're embarrassing yourself. / That's the hottest gossip site in DC. / Yeah? 'Lifting the lid on the Inter-agency Softball League'? 'Face to Face with America's Wind Tycoons.' Wow. This is some seriously butter knife-dull shit, man.
jokes.setup: Jonah is checking his phone/device and Dan looks over at his website.
jokes.punchline: Dan reads out actual WestWingMan headlines — 'Inter-agency Softball League,' 'America's Wind Tycoons' — which are so spectacularly dull they indict the 'hottest gossip site in DC' claim instantly. 'Butter knife-dull' is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent comedy writing. The actual WestWingMan headlines are perfectly chosen — they're not just boring, they're specifically, precisely boring in ways that feel researched. 'Inter-agency Softball League' and 'America's Wind Tycoons' are the kind of specificity that elevates a joke from generic to great. 'Butter knife-dull' is a strong original simile. And it pays off the entire WestWingMan setup. Rewatch bonus because you can spot the contradiction with Jonah's later reveal.

jokes.text: It's you, isn't it? / Who told you that? / You just did, you dummy.
jokes.setup: Dan has deduced Jonah runs WestWingMan and accuses him directly.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's denial ('Who told you that?') is itself the confirmation, and Dan points this out — 'You just did, you dummy' — the oldest trick in the book, and Jonah falls for it.
jokes.explanation: Classic trap-and-confirm structure, but character integration is strong — of course Jonah falls for this. 'You just did, you dummy' is the most satisfying possible punchline: simple, direct, and slightly condescending. The joke is old but executed cleanly.

jokes.text: Now if you will excuse me, I am at a wedding, and women at a wedding are like ripe fruit ready to drop. And I am a sex wasp.
jokes.setup: Jonah is wrapping up his conversation with Dan at the wedding.
jokes.punchline: 'I am a sex wasp' — a self-description so specific, bizarre, and unappealing that it transcends normal braggadocio into something genuinely absurd. The fruit/drop metaphor sets up a conventional enough image; 'sex wasp' completely derails it.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of Veep's great single lines. 'I am a sex wasp' is pure Jonah — delusional, aggressive, strangely specific, and accidentally more off-putting than any normal boast. A wasp is an UNPLEASANT insect associated with stinging and being unwanted, which makes it a perfect accidental self-own. The word 'sex' in front of 'wasp' creates an absurdist compound noun that has never existed before and yet feels completely inevitable for this character. It is instantly, permanently quotable. This ranks with Veep's best character lines.

jokes.text: Gonna wash those paws, big guy? / Are you kidding? My pheromones make bitches moan. I'm gonna leave a trail right back to my apartment.
jokes.setup: Dan notices Jonah heading from the bathroom without washing his hands.
jokes.punchline: Jonah explains he doesn't wash his hands because his pheromones are so powerful they attract women — he's going to 'leave a trail' of pheromones back to his apartment like a slug.
jokes.explanation: Direct escalation of the sex wasp joke. The pheromone logic is insane but internally consistent with Jonah's worldview. 'Leave a trail right back to my apartment' evokes the image of a disgusting trail — which accidentally describes Jonah perfectly. The grossness is the point.

jokes.text: Look at us. You pretending to be me signing a book I didn't even write. That's politics in a nutsack.
jokes.setup: Ben and Selina are watching Gary sign books on Selina's behalf at the Iowa event.
jokes.punchline: 'That's politics in a nutsack' — a compressed metaphor for political inauthenticity that reduces the entire system to something anatomically undignified.
jokes.explanation: Strong political cynicism delivered with maximum economy. 'In a nutsack' as the reduction of politics is a deliberately crass but precise substitution for 'in a nutshell.' The content supports the form — inauthenticity described through vulgar compression. Very quotable.

jokes.text: It's so full of shit, there's a colon right smack-dab in the middle.
jokes.setup: Ben is asked what he thinks of the book title 'Our Next American Journey.'
jokes.punchline: He says it's so full of shit that there's literally a colon in it — a pun on the punctuation mark (colon) and the anatomical colon, both connected to shit.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding joke. The colon pun operates on three levels simultaneously: (1) the book title literally has a colon in it, (2) 'colon' is the anatomical structure associated with shit, (3) the phrase 'full of shit' is being proven through punctuation. This is the kind of joke where the setup and punchline are the same fact viewed differently — that's exceptional comedic architecture. The precision of 'right smack-dab in the middle' makes it even better. This is an 8+ caliber joke without question.

jokes.text: Footsteps to the Future... Red, White and You... Yes Hands of Our Children. / What? / It's like a massacre or something.
jokes.setup: Selina is listing rejected book titles that her team suggested.
jokes.punchline: The titles are escalatingly awful — 'Yes Hands of Our Children' in particular is so incoherent Ben compares it to a massacre. The joke is the list-as-escalation and Ben's horrified reaction.
jokes.explanation: 'Yes Hands of Our Children' is a great piece of comedy writing — it sounds like it was generated by a random political phrase generator that malfunctioned. 'Footsteps to the Future' and 'Red, White and You' are bad but recognizable; 'Yes Hands of Our Children' is deranged. Ben's 'It's like a massacre' is the right reaction.

jokes.text: I was so busy listening to these stupid fucks, I didn't listen to this voice in my own head saying, 'This is dog shit. Selina, this is complete dog shit. Don't step in it, don't... You just stepped in it.'
jokes.setup: Selina is reflecting on how she ended up with a terrible book title despite knowing it was bad.
jokes.punchline: Her internal monologue is dramatized — the voice tries to warn her ('Don't step in it') and then immediately acknowledges she already stepped in it. The escalation from warning to post-facto condemnation in real-time is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Strong self-aware character moment. The structure of warning → acknowledgment of failure within the same internal monologue ('Don't step in it, don't... You just stepped in it') is tight and gets the self-sabotage of political figures. The repetition of 'dog shit' with 'complete' as intensifier is good comic writing.

jokes.text: Eighteen months is an entire pregnancy with another entire pregnancy tacked onto the end of it.
jokes.setup: Ben tries to reassure Selina that 18 months of campaigning is nothing.
jokes.punchline: Selina retorts that 18 months is 'an entire pregnancy with another entire pregnancy tacked onto the end of it' — a characteristically dramatic but precise calculation.
jokes.explanation: Selina's hyperbole is characteristically precise — she doesn't just say '18 months is forever,' she measures it in pregnancies. The addition of 'with another entire pregnancy tacked onto the end' is the escalating specificity that makes it Selina rather than anyone. Good, consistent character voice.

jokes.text: I could be your midwife. You know, get my hands dirty?
jokes.setup: Ben is pitching himself as a strategy adviser following Selina's pregnancy metaphor for the campaign.
jokes.punchline: He extends the pregnancy metaphor into 'I could be your midwife' — which is simultaneously an offer of help and an extremely undignified image for the Chief of Staff, made worse by 'get my hands dirty.'
jokes.explanation: Good callback extension of the pregnancy metaphor. Ben adopting 'midwife' as his role is both self-deprecating and slightly disgusting — 'get my hands dirty' is a phrase that takes on a very specific meaning in a midwifery context. Works as an escalation of the metaphor.

jokes.text: Um, could you go sit in that chair over there?
jokes.setup: Ben has just offered to be Selina's 'midwife,' presumably standing too close and being physically overpresent.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is not to engage with the metaphor but to physically relocate him — treating the Chief of Staff like a piece of furniture that's been placed wrong.
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina power move disguised as a practical request. She doesn't argue with his idea, she just rearranges the person. It's a dismissal that doesn't look like a dismissal — pure character comedy.

jokes.text: All right, let's go! Let's go meet and grieve. / We'll meet Ericsson, and we'll grieve little Ricky.
jokes.setup: Ben and Selina are planning to attend Cowgill's funeral to meet the campaign strategist Ericsson.
jokes.punchline: 'Meet and grieve' — a pun on 'meet and greet' that acknowledges they're exploiting a funeral for networking, compressing both the cynicism and the wordplay into one phrase.
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire compressed into a pun. 'Meet and grieve' earns its wordplay because it's simultaneously a pun AND an accurate description of what they're doing — networking at a funeral. The follow-up 'grieve little Ricky' extends it without belaboring it. Economy and structure are the strengths.

jokes.text: Yeah, God rest his tiny soul. / I know. You know, I heard that dog picked him up and shook him really bad.
jokes.setup: Selina and Ben are paying perfunctory respects to the late Congressman Cowgill as they plot to network at his funeral.
jokes.punchline: Ben adds that 'that dog picked him up and shook him really bad' — the specificity of the death involving a dog picking him up (possible given his 4'11" stature) is both horrifying and absurdly funny.
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy. The callback to Cowgill's extreme shortness pays off here — a dog picking him up and shaking him is only plausible because of the height jokes. This is earned dark comedy that uses established character detail rather than random cruelty. The phrase 'really bad' is perfect understatement — 'shook him really bad' as cause of death, delivered conversationally.

jokes.text: This is my last wedding. Come on.
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to organize the wedding photo and everyone is causing chaos.
jokes.punchline: 'This is my last wedding' — either a declaration that he plans to stay married forever, OR an expression of exasperation that has dark implications, OR just a stressed man saying something unintentionally funny.
jokes.explanation: Small but effective throwaway line. The ambiguity of 'my last wedding' works on the audience slightly differently depending on interpretation. Not a standout joke but good rhythm-comedy.

jokes.text: Gary's escalated cover story: 'The senator, who I mentioned earlier... his problems have become even bigger.'
jokes.setup: Gary had previously invented a fictional senator with problems to explain Selina's absence; now she's not coming back
jokes.punchline: The fictional senator's fictional problems have now grown — Gary deepening the lie rather than abandoning it

jokes.text: Jonah, what's the point? You don't show up in photographs.
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to get into the wedding photo despite not being invited.
jokes.punchline: Dan's dismissal isn't 'you're not in the wedding party' — it's 'you don't show up in photographs' — a vampire joke so casual it lands as deadpan fact.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding joke. Comparing Jonah to a vampire — with the implication he doesn't appear in photographs — is delivered so casually by Dan that it works as deadpan rather than setup-punchline. The economy is exceptional: no explanation, no wink, just the flat statement. It's the confidence of delivery that makes it hit. This is at the 8+ caliber: could only be said about/to Jonah, perfectly economical, instantly quotable.

jokes.text: Shit on my tits. / Maddox just resigned.
jokes.setup: The team is mid-wedding photo when Amy's secret spare phone goes off with breaking news.
jokes.punchline: Amy's exclamation — 'Shit on my tits' — is so specifically vulgar and so utterly wrong for a wedding photo moment that it works as both expletive and comedy.
jokes.explanation: 'Shit on my tits' is a memorably specific profanity — it has the Veep quality of being crude in an unexpected anatomical way. The juxtaposition with a wedding photo is the structural joke. Quotability is high as a piece of Veep invective.

jokes.text: Okay, you can have your phones for five minutes, guys. The Wi-Fi password is 'Starboard135A.'
jokes.setup: Wendy has been strictly enforcing the phone-bowl rule at her wedding. Maddox has just resigned.
jokes.punchline: Wendy — a reporter — immediately abandons her phone ban and gives out the Wi-Fi password the moment real news breaks. Her professional instincts override her wedding day preferences instantly.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. The entire phone bowl sequence was setup for this: Wendy, who enforced the rule most strictly, is the first to break it. The specific Wi-Fi password ('Starboard135A') delivered immediately is the perfect detail — she already had it ready. This is rewarding for attentive viewers and works as a statement about journalism overriding personal life.

jokes.text: 'Team Veep goes into meltdown as Sec Def Maddox news breaks.' And upload the money shot.
jokes.setup: Jonah has covertly photographed the Veep team panicking and is live-blogging it.
jokes.punchline: The WestWingMan headline is perfectly chosen — 'meltdown' is accurate but devastating, and 'upload the money shot' is Jonah treating a political crisis photo as pornography.
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the WestWingMan setup. 'Money shot' is characteristically vulgar Jonah-speak applied to political gossip photography. The headline is exactly what WestWingMan would write.

jokes.text: I hate how he learned English from pornography.
jokes.setup: Jonah says 'upload the money shot' about the crisis photo.
jokes.punchline: Dan's instant response — 'I hate how he learned English from pornography' — frames Jonah's crude vocabulary as a linguistic education problem rather than just bad taste.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest one-liners. The construction 'I hate how he learned English from pornography' is brilliant — it treats Jonah's crude vocabulary as a developmental fact rather than a choice, which is both funnier and more insulting. 'Learned English FROM pornography' is the key — not 'talks like he watches pornography' but 'this is where his English comes from.' Economy, originality, and character integration all at 8+. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Stop flapping your vestigial limbs around. The line on Maddox is one more old man has gone off to play chess in the park.
jokes.setup: Selina is managing the Maddox crisis and her team is panicking.
jokes.punchline: She dismisses the staff's panic with 'flapping your vestigial limbs' — an evolutionary insult implying they have useless appendages — then delivers the official line comparing Maddox to a retired old man playing chess.
jokes.explanation: 'Flapping your vestigial limbs' is exceptional — it's a scientific term used as an insult, implying her staff are evolutionary dead ends with useless appendages. This is Selina at her most viciously precise. 'One more old man has gone off to play chess in the park' as the official spin line is also very good — it's dismissive, slightly condescending, and completely at odds with the reality of a Defense Secretary resignation. Both halves earn their scores.

jokes.text: And somebody find out who WestWingMan is. FYI, it may not be his real name.
jokes.setup: Selina is issuing rapid-fire crisis management orders.
jokes.punchline: The parenthetical 'FYI, it may not be his real name' — deadpanning an observation so obvious it shouldn't need saying, but she says it anyway because she genuinely doesn't know.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels simultaneously: (1) it's funny that she says something so obvious, and (2) the audience knows Jonah is WestWingMan, so her obliviousness is dramatic irony. 'It may not be his real name' delivered as a genuine helpful hint is the perfect deadpan.

jokes.text: Dan, listen. We're going to have to do something if Maddox is actually gonna run. / Yes, ma'am, yes. I was voted Most Likely To Do Something Now in my class yearbook.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Dan they need to act on the Maddox situation.
jokes.punchline: Dan responds that he was voted 'Most Likely To Do Something Now' in his class yearbook — a completely made-up/absurd award that simultaneously mocks yearbook culture AND reveals Dan's ego.
jokes.explanation: 'Most Likely To Do Something Now' is a beautifully constructed absurd award. It sounds real enough but means absolutely nothing — perfectly deflating Dan's self-image. His citing it sincerely is the character comedy; the award itself is the absurdist comedy. Both layers work.

jokes.text: the closest you'll get to a political career will be selling Nixon masks at a Halloween shop
jokes.setup: Selina is threatening Richard not to reveal that POTUS isn't running and that she's planning to.
jokes.punchline: The specific penalty for blurting is being condemned to sell Nixon masks at a Halloween shop — a very precise, undignified fate that connects political failure to the most embarrassing commercial application of political imagery.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Nixon masks at a Halloween shop is what elevates this beyond a generic threat. It's not just 'your career is over' — it's a precise, humiliating vision of what failure looks like. Nixon as the symbol of political failure is well-chosen.

jokes.text: POTUS isn't seeking re-election? / I said don't blurt. You blurted. / You blurted about running. / You're running?
jokes.setup: Selina warns Richard not to blurt anything about POTUS's plans. Richard immediately asks about POTUS not seeking re-election.
jokes.punchline: They all start accusing each other of blurting while continuing to blurt — a cascade of blurting about blurting, with each accusation revealing more information.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural comedy — the blurting escalation where each attempt to stop blurting causes more blurting is a comedy of errors that builds cleanly. Richard's immediate blurt after being told not to blurt is the foundation, but the cascade of mutual accusations is what makes it great. Classic farce structure in a modern political setting.

jokes.text: Ben, can you not keep a cat in a bag for one fuckin' second? / Now we're gonna have to kill him.
jokes.setup: Ben has revealed sensitive information to Richard through the blurting cascade.
jokes.punchline: 'Now we're gonna have to kill him' — delivered presumably as dark humor about Richard knowing too much, a mob-movie cliché applied to political information control.
jokes.explanation: 'Keep a cat in a bag' as a variant of 'let the cat out of the bag' is a good inversion. 'Now we're gonna have to kill him' lands as dark political humor. Both halves work; 'kill him' is funnier because Richard is still standing right there.

jokes.text: Ma'am, if you need any help with your campaign, I'm real good in a high-pressure situation. / Really? In what sense 'good'?
jokes.setup: Richard, having just learned all the secrets, immediately volunteers for the campaign.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response — 'Really? In what sense good?' — is the perfect beat. She doesn't dismiss him; she genuinely wants clarification on the definition of 'good,' implying she's seen no evidence of it.
jokes.explanation: 'In what sense good?' is a beautifully calibrated put-down that masquerades as a genuine question. It's more devastating than a flat rejection because it implies that Selina has considered all possible senses of 'good' and found them lacking. Pure Selina.

jokes.text: I was all over that book line thing. / Yeah. I tell you what, get the driver to turn the air conditioning on, okay? 'Cause I'm boiling up here. / I can do that! / How do I do that? / Just open the door while we're moving, climb under the car like Indiana Jones, pop up on the hood and write a note on the windshield.
jokes.setup: Richard claims he was 'all over' the book signing as evidence he's good at his job. Selina asks him to do a simple task — turn on the AC.
jokes.punchline: Richard immediately doesn't know how to do it. Ben provides absurdly elaborate instructions (Indiana Jones-style car maneuver) to accomplish what any adult could do by asking the driver.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke. The Indiana Jones instructions are specific, visual, and increasingly absurd — 'climb under the car like Indiana Jones, pop up on the hood and write a note on the windshield' is a perfectly detailed bit of absurdism. And Richard appears to take it seriously, which makes it land harder. Ben's delivery of genuinely helpful-sounding nonsense is great character work.

jokes.text: Texting behind your back? Jesus. / You Hendrix texting? / Just staying ahead. That's what a good campaign manager does. / No.
jokes.setup: Dan catches Amy texting with her hands behind her back at the wedding.
jokes.punchline: 'You Hendrix texting?' — a made-up term for texting behind your back, compared to guitar virtuosity — and then Amy's flat 'No.' when Dan insists staying ahead is what a good campaign manager does.
jokes.explanation: 'Hendrix texting' is a good invented term — the comparison to guitar virtuosity applied to covert phone use is the right level of absurdism. Amy's flat 'No.' in response to Dan's self-justification is the real punchline — both competing for campaign manager while accusing each other of being unsuitable.

jokes.text: You guys are just like two little pretty Easter eggs. I'd love to crack you open. / Hey, Hepatitis-J.
jokes.setup: Jonah is commenting on the Dan-Amy rivalry.
jokes.punchline: Jonah compares them to Easter eggs he wants to crack open — a disturbingly physical metaphor from someone who thinks he's being charming. Dan immediately retaliates with 'Hepatitis-J' — a medical insult that combines Jonah's initial 'J' with a disease.
jokes.explanation: 'Hepatitis-J' is an instant classic Veep insult — the naming convention of hepatitis strains applied to Jonah, with his initial. It implies Jonah is a disease AND that he's a lesser strain (not A, B, or C, but J). The Easter egg line from Jonah is also good — the image of wanting to crack them open is both threatening and absurd.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Hepatitis-J.'
jokes.setup: Jonah approaches Dan at the wedding
jokes.punchline: Hepatitis-J — a Jonah Ryan-specific variation of a disease name, implying he is a pathogen
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum damage. 'Hepatitis-J' works because it takes a real medical category, inserts Jonah's initial, and thus calls him a disease — specifically a liver disease spread through dirty contact. It's both an elegant insult structure and deeply character-specific. Among the most quotable moments of the episode.

jokes.text: 'You don't think you should go fist a chimp?'
jokes.setup: Dan asks if Jonah should maybe take down his post
jokes.punchline: Jonah's counterproposal — a sexually violent act involving a chimpanzee
jokes.explanation: Jonah's instinct to escalate immediately to zoological sexual violence is characteristically unhinged. The specificity of 'chimp' over any generic animal is what makes it Jonah.

jokes.text: How's that pic you posted on your blog going, huh? / It's a hashtag hurricane, bitch tits.
jokes.setup: Dan asks about the impact of Jonah's photo post showing the Veep team in meltdown.
jokes.punchline: 'It's a hashtag hurricane, bitch tits' — Jonah's triumphant description of viral social media traction, delivered with personal insult appended as punctuation.
jokes.explanation: 'Hashtag hurricane' as a coinage for viral trending is good original phrasing. 'Bitch tits' as appended insult is distinctly Jonah — it's gratuitous and specific. The combination makes a memorably Jonah sentence.

jokes.text: You don't think you should go fist a chimp?
jokes.setup: Dan suggests Jonah should maybe take down the post.
jokes.punchline: Jonah responds with a suggestion that Dan go fist a chimp — a counterattack so aggressively specific and disgusting it functions as pure Jonah character comedy.
jokes.explanation: Characteristically Jonah — the insult is specific, violent, and involves an animal. 'Fist a chimp' has the quality of something a 14-year-old who just learned swear words would say, which is exactly Jonah's register. The escalation from 'maybe take that down?' to 'go fist a chimp' is the comedy.

jokes.text: Yeah, yeah. Make sure you do that before Google caches it, because once that happens, it's on there till the end of the world, which will also probably be your fault.
jokes.setup: Jonah agrees to take down the post.
jokes.punchline: Dan tacks on 'which will also probably be your fault' after mentioning the end of the world — a casual, absolute condemnation of Jonah as responsible for the apocalypse.
jokes.explanation: Strong parenthetical comedy — the end-of-world coda is delivered so casually it's almost more devastating than an aggressive accusation. 'Which will also probably be your fault' implies a long track record of Jonah causing disasters, culminating logically in the apocalypse.

jokes.text: What's Google's number? / I don't know. Ask Jeeves.
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to figure out how to prevent Google from caching his post.
jokes.punchline: Jonah asks for Google's phone number (revealing profound tech illiteracy), and Dan responds 'Ask Jeeves' — directing him to the most famously defunct search engine as a dismissal.
jokes.explanation: Two-layer joke: Jonah's tech illiteracy ('What's Google's number?') is the setup; 'Ask Jeeves' is the perfect punchline because it simultaneously mocks Jonah AND is itself a useless answer (Ask Jeeves no longer exists). Dan is being unhelpfully correct: the answer to 'how do I find information?' is 'use a search engine,' but the specific search engine he names is dead. Both parts work.

jokes.text: Okay, that's Isaac Denisov from Change.org. She hates him. / This way, ma'am. You hate him. / Oh, yeah. That's the first one you've got right today.
jokes.setup: At the funeral, Ben spots someone Selina dislikes and steers her away.
jokes.punchline: Selina's confirmation — 'That's the first one you've got right today' — is a casual brutal assessment of Ben's entire performance, delivered as a throwaway.
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina put-down structure — she uses agreement as an insult. 'That's the first one you've got right today' is both confirmation and comprehensive condemnation of Ben's competence. The casualness is perfect.

jokes.text: He's the bubonic fuckin' plague of this party.
jokes.setup: Ben is explaining why they need to avoid Blake Stewart, a failed former candidate, at the funeral.
jokes.punchline: Calling Blake Stewart 'the bubonic fucking plague of this party' — a medieval disease as a metaphor for political toxicity — is characteristically hyperbolic Ben.
jokes.explanation: Good hyperbole but fairly standard Veep-style invective. 'Bubonic plague' is a strong image but not among the episode's most original metaphors. The 'fucking' insertion is well-placed rhythmically.

jokes.text: Exciting, then exhausting. / Ah. / Then I died. Joking. Kinda.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Blake what a campaign was like for him.
jokes.punchline: 'Then I died. Joking. Kinda.' — the 'kinda' withdrawal of the joke makes it land harder, implying the campaign effectively killed something in him, or that he genuinely isn't sure he's joking.
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure (Exciting → Exhausting → Then I died) is perfectly economical. 'Joking. Kinda.' is the kicker that makes it resonate — the half-retraction is funnier than either full commitment or full deflection. One of the better jokes in the funeral sequence.

jokes.text: I don't hear that a lot.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Blake 'It's good speaking with you' as she tries to escape the conversation.
jokes.punchline: Blake's 'I don't hear that a lot' — completely flat delivery, no self-pity, just a statement of fact about how people don't enjoy talking to him — is a beautifully sad-funny beat.
jokes.explanation: Four words doing maximum work. 'I don't hear that a lot' in response to a polite exit is perfectly calibrated — Blake's self-awareness about his social toxicity, delivered without any plea for sympathy, is genuinely funny and slightly heartbreaking. Economy score is near-perfect.

jokes.text: The prime minister of Scotland is on the line right now. / It sounds important.
jokes.setup: Selina is trapped at the funeral. Gary finds a way to extract her from the Blake conversation.
jokes.punchline: 'The prime minister of Scotland is on the line right now. It sounds important.' — delivered to rescue Selina from an awkward conversation. Scotland doesn't have a prime minister (it has a First Minister); the fakeness of the excuse and the tacked-on 'it sounds important' is transparently bad.
jokes.explanation: The rewatch bonus: 'Prime Minister of Scotland' is either Gary's ignorance (Scotland has a First Minister, not a PM — GB has a PM) or a completely invented rescue call. Either way, the gaffe signals how bad the cover story is. 'It sounds important' as qualifier is the Gary-specific anxious over-sell.

jokes.text: Well, you know what? We could consummate this marriage right here. No one would notice or give a shit. / We could. Or? / We could check our phones.
jokes.setup: Mike and Wendy are having a private moment at their wedding.
jokes.punchline: The romantic alternative to consummating the marriage is checking their phones — Wendy's offer of phone-checking as equivalent to sex on their wedding day is the joke. The 'Or?' anticipating something romantic, met with 'We could check our phones' is the deflation.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff to the entire phone bowl arc. After all the struggle to give up phones, Mike and Wendy's first romantic opportunity becomes a choice between sex and phones — and phones are presented as a genuine alternative. Character integration is strong: Wendy the reporter can't fully disengage.

jokes.text: Madam Vice President? / Yes? / I was Rick's nephew. / Are you kidding me? / This is Cowgill's nephew. / If they tell her who they are, you don't have to say anything.
jokes.setup: A man at the funeral introduces himself as Rick Cowgill's nephew to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Are you kidding me?' reveals she has no idea what to say to real bereaved people; Ben's aside 'if they tell her who they are, you don't have to say anything' is the cold political calculus applied to human grief.
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'Are you kidding me?' as her response to meeting a grieving nephew is pure Selina — she's only horrified because it puts her in an awkward position. Ben's instant coaching — 'if they tell her who they are, you don't have to say anything' — reveals the brutal political calculus of grieving. Dark cringe.

jokes.text: Cowgill's nephew appears: 'I was Rick's nephew.' / 'Are you kidding me?' / 'This is Cowgill's nephew. / If they tell her who they are, you don't have to say anything.'
jokes.setup: A person approaches Selina at the funeral and identifies themselves
jokes.punchline: Gary's whispered coaching — 'if they tell her who they are, you don't have to say anything' — reveals Selina's standard operating procedure at condolence events
jokes.explanation: Gary's system for getting Selina through condolences — if they introduce themselves, she doesn't need to know their name — reveals both the scale of her public responsibilities and the transactional nature of political empathy.

jokes.text: I was gonna actually ask the Vice President if she would like to say something. / No, it's fine. / I'm a terrible speaker anyway. / No, you're not. / Yeah. That's how I lost 49 states.
jokes.setup: Blake is asked to speak at Cowgill's funeral and deflects to Selina, who deflects back. Blake says he's a terrible speaker.
jokes.punchline: 'That's how I lost 49 states' — Blake's dry self-deprecation about his landslide loss, delivered as a simple explanation of his terrible speaking.
jokes.explanation: 'That's how I lost 49 states' is a good self-deprecating line with the specificity of '49' making it worse — not just a loss, but a near-total wipeout. Delivered in response to being told he's NOT a terrible speaker makes the disagreement-as-evidence funny.

jokes.text: Hey. What's going on, man? What's crackin? / We got POTUS incoming? / May I see your White House pass, Mr. Ryan? / Uh, yes, sir. Absolutely. There you are. / Leave. / But I work here. / Not anymore.
jokes.setup: Jonah approaches the White House/West Wing entrance confidently.
jokes.punchline: After showing his pass, he's told 'Leave' — 'But I work here' — 'Not anymore.' The speed of his firing, delivered as three flat words, is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The three-word firing ('Leave / But I work here / Not anymore') is structurally perfect — the exchange is compressed to its absolute minimum. 'Not anymore' lands hard because it's so flat. Jonah's 'What's crackin?' opener makes the subsequent firing more brutal by contrast.

jokes.text: Sir, I think I've temporarily lost your meaning.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just been told to leave and that he doesn't work there anymore.
jokes.punchline: 'I think I've temporarily lost your meaning' — Jonah's inability to process being fired, expressed as a temporary communication breakdown rather than the reality that he's been terminated.
jokes.explanation: 'Temporarily lost your meaning' is an excellent formulation — it's bureaucratic language applied to an emotional crisis, and the word 'temporarily' is doing all the comic work (implying this is a communications issue, not a reality issue). Very quotable and very Jonah: he processes rejection as a technical failure he can fix.

jokes.text: The party is over, Mr. WestWingMan. There is a dead guy in the pool, and that dead guy is you.
jokes.setup: The West Wing is confronting Jonah about his WestWingMan blog.
jokes.punchline: 'There is a dead guy in the pool, and that dead guy is you' — mixing swimming pool/political metaphors, the 'dead guy in the pool' is evocative but slightly confused, which makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: A good if slightly confused metaphor. 'Dead guy in the pool' is vivid but what pool? Political? Swimming? The ambiguity is probably intentional, making the threat feel more sinister. The double 'that dead guy is you' lands as an effective threat-joke.

jokes.text: One, you were running a news blog while working in office. / It was gossip-tainment.
jokes.setup: Jonah is being formally charged with his WestWingMan crimes.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defensive clarification — 'It was gossip-tainment' — attempts to reframe his blog as a genre category that somehow makes it acceptable, which makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's insistence on 'gossip-tainment' as a category that supersedes ethics is perfect character work — the callback to his earlier use of the term shows this is his actual worldview, not a desperate defense. The made-up portmanteau is consistently applied, which makes the character more coherent even as the logic falls apart.

jokes.text: Two, you posted a photo, which roused the suspicions of the press. It has been forwarded to every hack in DC. / That wasn't me. And I deleted it. / Which made it even worse!
jokes.setup: Jonah defends himself by saying he deleted the photo.
jokes.punchline: 'Which made it even worse!' — the deleting of the evidence made the situation worse, which is both logically sound and absurdly counterintuitive from Jonah's perspective.
jokes.explanation: 'Which made it even worse!' is a solid escalation beat — the defense becoming evidence is a classic comedy structure. Works as part of the Jonah firing sequence's escalating absurdity.

jokes.text: So as a direct result of your actions, the President of the United States is bringing forward the announcement to today that he is not running for re-election.
jokes.setup: Jonah's gossip blog post has caused a political crisis.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Jonah — the most incompetent character — has inadvertently shaped U.S. political history through his WestWingMan post is the absurdist payoff of the entire episode.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural payoff joke — Jonah, who is fired from the West Wing for incompetence, is simultaneously the person who forced the most significant political announcement of the Selina Meyer storyline. His incompetence has accidentally accelerated history. This is the absurdist through-line of the episode paying off.

jokes.text: Sir, please, don't take this away from me. The West Wing is part of my DNA and vice verse. / Okay, this is a teachable moment, and I think... / You're embarrassing yourself.
jokes.setup: Jonah is pleading not to be fired from the West Wing.
jokes.punchline: 'The West Wing is part of my DNA and vice verse' — Jonah mangling 'vice versa' into 'vice verse' while making a grand declaration about his identity, followed by Terrence's devastating 'You're embarrassing yourself.'
jokes.explanation: 'Vice verse' instead of 'vice versa' during a heartfelt plea is excruciating cringe comedy — Jonah undermines his own passionate declaration with a malapropism. 'You're embarrassing yourself' is the perfect response because it addresses both the plea AND the language error simultaneously. High character integration: only Jonah would do this.

jokes.text: Sir, I don't have anything else in my life. / He really doesn't. / See? Exactly. Thank you, Dan. / Fuck you, Dan!
jokes.setup: Jonah makes a desperate plea that he has nothing else in his life besides the West Wing job.
jokes.punchline: Dan validates Jonah's claim ('He really doesn't'), which Terrence uses as evidence against Jonah ('See? Exactly. Thank you, Dan.'), then Jonah turns on Dan. The accidental confirmation from Dan is the knife in the back.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best comedic exchanges. The four-beat structure is flawless: (1) Jonah's pathetic appeal, (2) Dan's casual validation that destroys him, (3) the authority figure using Dan's testimony against Jonah, (4) Jonah's betrayed 'Fuck you, Dan!' The economy is perfect — every word advances the comedy. Dan's betrayal is entirely in character: he confirms Jonah's misery because it's true, not because he's being malicious, which makes it worse. Structure score approaches 9.

jokes.text: That bag of wrist slits got the nomination? With that face and that personality?
jokes.setup: Selina has survived her conversation with Blake Stewart, the failed candidate.
jokes.punchline: 'That bag of wrist slits' — a devastatingly dark description of Blake Stewart's effect on people (presumably so depressing he drives people to self-harm) delivered with genuine disbelief that he got a nomination.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Veep insult. 'Bag of wrist slits' is genuinely original — it implies this person is so depressing they trigger self-destruction in others, compressed into a portable container. This is dark comedy at the level of the best Veep insults. The specificity ('bag' — not just 'wrist slits,' but a contained collection of them) elevates it from mere darkness into precise craft. Highly quotable and original.

jokes.text: You think I got here just 'cause I got $50 million in the bank and this amazing ass?
jokes.setup: Selina is asserting that she doesn't need a big campaign team, she's capable on her own.
jokes.punchline: She lists $50 million and 'this amazing ass' as among her qualifications — the latter delivered with total sincerity, which makes the inclusion of her physical appearance alongside her bank account both funnier and more Selina.
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecation-by-bragging. The joke is that Selina lists her qualifications and 'this amazing ass' is one of them, delivered without irony. Character integration is high — Selina's vanity and her genuine self-belief coexist perfectly here.

jokes.text: He was an avid fisherman. / And he collected beer labels. / What are you gonna do with that?
jokes.setup: Selina demands facts about Cowgill from her team so she can speak at his funeral with no preparation.
jokes.punchline: The best they can offer is 'avid fisherman' and 'collected beer labels' — completely useless eulogy material. Selina's 'What are you gonna do with that?' reveals she's going to have to work with nothing.
jokes.explanation: Setup for the eulogy callback — the beer labels and fisherman details will be literally incorporated into Selina's eulogy, making this moment funnier in retrospect. 'What are you gonna do with that?' is the right reaction and sets up the payoff perfectly.

jokes.text: Well, I was planning on keeping this brief. In fact, I was gonna say 'short,' but I know how sensitive Rick was about his height.
jokes.setup: Blake is opening his eulogy at Cowgill's funeral.
jokes.punchline: He catches himself almost saying 'short,' pivots to explain he won't say it because of Cowgill's height sensitivity — but in explaining this, makes exactly the joke he was avoiding.
jokes.explanation: Classic self-defeating setup — Blake explicitly avoids the height joke and then tells the height joke anyway. The structure is clean: catch → pivot → inadvertent delivery. The eulogy context makes the height joke darker and therefore funnier.

jokes.text: You think you've seen the end of Jonah Ryan? You haven't even seen the start of Jonah Ryan! I'm leaving here with my head held high and my nuts hanging low on your mom's chin, Martin.
jokes.setup: Jonah is being escorted out of the West Wing after being fired.
jokes.punchline: His defiant exit speech climaxes with 'my nuts hanging low on your mom's chin, Martin' — a juvenile sexual insult delivered at maximum intensity during what should be a dignified exit.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. The speech arc — 'You haven't seen the start of Jonah Ryan' into 'my nuts hanging low on your mom's chin' — is perfectly structured as a self-undermining rhetoric. Jonah builds to what he thinks is a defiant climax and delivers a your-mom joke from 2003. 'Martin' as the specific target (we have no context for who Martin is) makes it even funnier — he's addressing someone specific in his final moment of disgrace. Pure Jonah in crisis: grandiose, crude, and self-defeating simultaneously. High character integration score is justified.

jokes.text: Rick's life reflects America. He was an avid fisherman. And as a congressman, gosh, did he know how to cast upstream, to anticipate it. And when he got that first bite, whoa, did he reel it in. He was a nuanced politician with a mighty big collection of beer labels.
jokes.setup: Selina is delivering an improvised eulogy for Cowgill using only the two facts she was given: 'avid fisherman' and 'collected beer labels.'
jokes.punchline: She has turned 'avid fisherman' into an extended political metaphor (casting upstream, anticipating, reeling in) and then landed on 'a mighty big collection of beer labels' as the capstone of his political legacy — an absurdly mundane legacy for a congressman.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best sustained comic sequences. The fishing-as-political-metaphor speech is beautiful improvised nonsense — every line sounds like genuine political oratory ('cast upstream, to anticipate it, reel it in') but means nothing. The pivot to 'a mighty big collection of beer labels' as the final legacy is the perfect deflation. The earnestness of Selina selling this complete nothing as profound tribute is elite Veep character comedy. Structure is near-flawless as a callback payoff. Earned comedy: we know exactly how little material she had and that knowledge makes every beat hit harder.

jokes.text: I'll be back. I'm gonna be back as the fucking president. Jonah Ryan, 2026! / That's a mid-terms year, Jonah. / Well, then, I'll change it!
jokes.setup: Jonah is being escorted out of the West Wing, continuing to threaten future vengeance.
jokes.punchline: He announces his presidential run for 2026 — a mid-term year. Corrected, he simply says 'I'll change it!' — implying he'll change the year, not adjust his plans.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding joke. 'Jonah Ryan, 2026!' being a mid-terms year is funny enough — but 'Well, then, I'll change it!' is the response of a man who is so delusional that he thinks the calendar year is the adjustable variable, not his ambition. He would literally change the election schedule rather than modify his plans. This is Jonah's character distilled to its essence: boundless delusion without any contact with reality. Economy and character integration are both near-perfect.

jokes.text: Well, I blew the fuckin' roof off of this church, didn't I? They loved that beer labels anecdote. Why would anyone collect those?
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered her improvised fisherman/beer-labels eulogy.
jokes.punchline: She's completely satisfied with herself ('blew the fuckin' roof off'), then immediately undercuts the eulogy's sincerity by admitting she has no idea why Cowgill collected beer labels — the 'anecdote' she told with such authority was meaningless to her.
jokes.explanation: Perfect coda to the eulogy sequence. 'Why would anyone collect those?' as an immediate post-eulogy question about the central 'anecdote' reveals Selina used a fact she found incomprehensible as the emotional capstone of a man's memorial. Her self-satisfaction ('blew the fuckin' roof off') makes the cognitive dissonance even funnier.

jokes.text: I'm never ambiguous, ma'am. Not even sexually.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Ben if he's saying what she thinks he's saying about POTUS's announcement.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm never ambiguous, ma'am. Not even sexually' — the addition of 'not even sexually' to a professional assurance of clarity is a throwaway that implies a lot about Ben's personal life while meaning nothing.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Not even sexually' is a brilliant addition — it's completely unexpected, opens a window into Ben's private life that immediately closes, and is delivered as a matter-of-fact professional assurance. The comedy is in the parenthetical: he's clarifying he means everything he says, in all contexts, including sexual contexts, which is information no one asked for and everyone is now slightly concerned about. Economy is near-perfect: five words do enormous character work.

jokes.text: Well, I don't like to toot my own horn, but... Yes, yes, I did. And just like that, Jonah is gone, forever.
jokes.setup: Dan reveals to Selina that it was his forwarding of Jonah's photo that triggered the whole chain of events leading to Jonah's firing and POTUS's announcement.
jokes.punchline: Dan takes credit with maximum false modesty — 'I don't like to toot my own horn, but...' — and then the celebratory 'And just like that, Jonah is gone, forever' as if he's eliminated an arch-nemesis.
jokes.explanation: Dan's self-congratulation is perfectly in character. The grandiosity of 'just like that, Jonah is gone, forever' is funny because Dan treats the removal of an annoying colleague as a victory equivalent to a military campaign. The rewatch bonus: we now understand the whole photo chain of causation.

jokes.text: Well, all my orgasms have come at once.
jokes.setup: Selina learns that POTUS is making his announcement, Jonah is fired, and her path to the presidency is clear.
jokes.punchline: 'All my orgasms have come at once' — Selina's expression of simultaneous political victories expressed through the most physically graphic metaphor possible, delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. 'All my orgasms have come at once' as an expression of political joy is both physically explicit and emotionally precise — it captures the simultaneous release of multiple tensions. It's also quintessentially Selina: she expresses political victories in the most personal, physical terms possible. The economy is near-perfect: seven words convey complete emotional state. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dan asks to be campaign manager; Selina says 'Oh, I've gotta go. Thank you, Dan.' — immediately ends the call
jokes.setup: Dan has orchestrated the Jonah destruction and now asks for his reward
jokes.punchline: Selina hangs up the instant Dan makes his ask — the 'Thank you, Dan' is exactly her 'Then you got married. And I love that story' energy

jokes.text: Book tour, day six. Cedar Rapids, here we come. / Oh, Jesus Christ. I hate politics.
jokes.setup: After all the excitement — POTUS's announcement, Jonah's firing, Selina's path to the presidency — Richard cheerfully reminds Selina that the book tour continues.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, Jesus Christ. I hate politics.' — coming immediately after Selina announced 'I love politics,' this reversal in under thirty seconds perfectly captures her mercurial relationship with her chosen profession.
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-closing joke. The reversal from 'I love politics' to 'I hate politics' in under 30 seconds, triggered by a reminder that real political work means Cedar Rapids on day six of a book tour, is a structural masterpiece. It's the thesis of the whole episode and of Veep itself: political ambition and political reality are constantly at war. The economy is exceptional — six words wrap the episode thematically. Richard's cheerful delivery of terrible news is the ideal trigger.

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, Vice President Selina Meyer! / I wanted to say a few words and speak very frankly. I was gonna say 'baldly,' but I know how sensitive Mike is about his hair.
jokes.setup: Selina video-calls into Mike's wedding reception to make a surprise appearance.
jokes.punchline: She opens with 'baldly' as a variation of 'frankly' — then explains she almost said it because of Mike's hair sensitivity, which is the same joke structure Blake used at the funeral about Cowgill's height.
jokes.explanation: Nice structural callback — Selina uses the exact same self-undermining joke structure Blake used at Cowgill's funeral, but applied to Mike's baldness. The rewatch bonus is recognizing the parallel. It also suggests Selina was paying more attention to Blake's speech than she let on.

jokes.text: Selina's speech at Mike's wedding: 'I was gonna say "baldly," but I know how sensitive Mike is about his hair.'
jokes.setup: Selina opens her wedding speech by saying she'll speak 'frankly'
jokes.punchline: The callback to her own eulogy punchline — 'baldly' instead of 'frankly' — applied to Mike's hair loss

jokes.text: We all know how much Mike loves his boat. And I'll tell you something. If he ever chose to fish, I know that he'd be able to cast upstream, to anticipate, and at that very first bite, reel it in. 'Cause that's the kind of guy that Mike is.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving a wedding toast to Mike and Wendy.
jokes.punchline: She uses the identical fishing-as-political-metaphor speech from Cowgill's eulogy — word for word — but applied to Mike, who apparently loves boats but has never fished. The complete recycling of a funeral speech as a wedding toast is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. The fishing speech callback is perfect comedy architecture: the audience remembers the eulogy, recognizes every word, and simultaneously processes that (1) Selina is reusing a funeral speech as a wedding toast, (2) the fishing metaphor was already meaningless in its original context, (3) Mike apparently loves boats but has never fished (making 'if he ever chose to fish' the most damning qualifier), and (4) Selina sells it with the same conviction. The fact that she got away with it at a funeral and is now getting away with it at a wedding — with minor adjustments — is the definitive statement about political speechmaking. Structural score approaches 10 because it is a perfect payoff to an established setup. This is legitimately great television comedy writing.

jokes.text: Some New Beginnings: Their Next American Journey!
jokes.setup: Selina is concluding her wedding toast.
jokes.punchline: She riffs on her own book title — 'Some New Beginnings: Their Next American Journey' — applying the hated, meaningless title to Mike and Wendy's marriage as a toast, weaponizing her own PR emptiness as sentimentality.
jokes.explanation: Strong episode-closing callback. Selina takes the book title she couldn't change, couldn't defend, and mocked privately — and turns it into a toast, finding the only use for it that actually makes emotional sense: applied to a real human milestone. It's both satire of political branding AND surprisingly touching, which is the best version of Veep comedy.

tmdb_overview: In Iowa, Selina courts possible caucus voters at a book signing for her new autobiography, while her team attends a staff wedding in D.C. As the day proceeds, she and her staff get word of a rumor that could affect the next election.
insight: Veep stumbles back to form with 78 jokes in 34 minutes, but impact underwhelms the density.
